MMA 616 · Managing Intelligence · Individual Final · submitted 2026-07-18

Edmonton Fiscal Pressure Navigator

Alejandro Cruz · An agentic workspace on Government of Alberta open data — Alberta Municipal Financial & Statistical Data (2018–2025) and Alberta CSD Population Estimates. Built and evaluated with Claude Code around a living spec (CLAUDE.md).

1 · The workspace 2 · Evidence of use 3 · Dashboard screenshots 4 · Evaluation — before and after 5 · Write-up 6 · Distinctness statement
Section 1 of 6

The workspace

The Edmonton Fiscal Pressure Navigator is a Claude Code workspace that answers one recurring question for one named user: an Edmonton municipal budget / financial-planning manager who, before a budget or capital-planning review, must decide which two or three financial pressure areas deserve deeper management attention. It turns eight annual Municipal Financial & Statistical Data workbooks and the Alberta CSD population estimates into at most three transparent, fully traceable pressure signals — never a score, never a recommendation.

The four parts

PartWhat it holds
Living SpecCLAUDE.md — the user, the decision, testable "what good looks like" (a certified gold example with tolerances), load-bearing rules (never use the financial workbooks' population field; never extract by column position; missing ≠ zero; cross-sectional evidence can never create a high-priority signal), scope refusals, and the governance boundary. Revised twice through use and criticism — the pressure-rules document it mandates went v1.0 → v1.1 → v1.2 under owner-approved change control.
KnowledgeThe nine raw workbooks connected as live files in data/raw/ (never modified — verified by byte size and tests), plus Knowledge/DATA_AND_KNOWLEDGE_ASSESSMENT.md, the codebook-equivalent: schema drift 42→51 worksheets, variable-code histories, the stale-population trap, name-matching hazards, zero-vs-blank semantics.
SkillsTwo Claude Skills with enforcing code checks, two scoped subagents, and three fixed pipelines (details below). Every capability took the simplest form that does the job; the connector slot is deliberately empty — local files in, local files out, no live external system is touched mid-analysis.
Governancegovernance/metric_dictionary.md (the only approved metrics, with formula + schedule + variable code + verified code history), municipality_crosswalk.csv (19 city rows — Edmonton 0098↔4811061 and Calgary 0046↔4806016 approved as population joins; the other 17 cities matched geo-scope-only for the context map, signed off in crosswalk_signoff.md), and pressure_rules.md (versioned thresholds with a change-control table; every amendment owner-approved and dated). Human gates: rules approval before any signal; brief approval before publishing; every critic finding triaged by the owner.

Folder structure

CLAUDE.md                     living spec (revised 2026-07-17: context map + methodology views)
PRODUCT.md                    product brief — brand, anti-references, design principles
data/raw/                     9 source workbooks + StatCan 2021 CSD boundaries + StatCan CPI table (18100005-eng.zip), read-only
data/curated/                 financial_long.csv · population.csv · metric_mart.csv/json · signals.json · alberta_geo.json
governance/                   metric_dictionary.md · municipality_crosswalk.csv · crosswalk_signoff.md · pressure_rules.md (v1.2)
pipeline/                     build_mart.py · detect_signals.py · build_geo.py (fixed pipelines)
.claude/skills/               metric-validation/ · pressure-detection/ (each with a blocking code check)
.claude/agents/               municipal-finance-analyst.md · independent-critic.md
dashboard/                    mockup.html (PLAN artifact) · index.html + data.js + geo.js + vendor/leaflet (five live views)
tests/                        57 pytest known-answer / quality / signal / geo / CPI tests
outputs/                      brief_2025.md · run_log.md · evaluation.md
Knowledge/                    data assessment + course material

Capability-to-form map (Day 2 discipline: verbs first, simplest form that works)

Job to be doneFormWhy that form
Inventory workbooks, extract approved codes, harmonize schemas, join population, compute metrics with full source trace Fixed pipeline — pipeline/build_mart.py Identical inputs must give identical outputs. Extraction is by worksheet + row-3 variable code, never column position; CODE read as text; blanks stay missing, zeros stay reported_zero.
Detect pressure signals Fixed pipeline — pipeline/detect_signals.py + Skill pressure-detection Signals come from one deterministic path applying the owner-approved rules; the skill makes any agent take that path and run the structural gate (check_signals.py) before presenting anything.
Validate every metric before use Skill metric-validation with validate_mart.py A recurring judgment that must hold every run. The check is a hard gate: tampering the gold value to 42.0 makes it exit 1 and block — verified, not assumed.
Match municipalities Governed data asset — the crosswalk CSV Matching rules are knowledge under review, not code. Only two matches are approved; peer per-capita medians are therefore structurally unavailable and labelled so.
Explain signals, draft the brief Subagent municipal-finance-analyst One distinct judgment (cautious narrative) worth isolating. It may not recompute metrics, may not recommend policy, and cannot remove the DRAFT marker — publishing is the owner's gate.
Criticize independently Subagent independent-critic The checker cannot be the author. Fresh context, banned from build history, briefed to attack the spec's promises and recompute numbers from raw with its own code. Ran twice (before/after).
Display the five views Native — static dashboard/index.html reading a generated data.js No live computation needed in the browser; heavy work happens once, offline. The Alberta map draws its bubbles from the generated geo.js over a Carto/OSM base-tile layer (presentation only) with one vendored library, Leaflet 1.9.4 — owner-approved 2026-07-18; no server.
Draw the Alberta context map Fixed pipeline — pipeline/build_geo.py Geometry from the owner-approved StatCan boundary file; per-city values recomputed with the mart’s own guards and hard-checked to reproduce the published medians and the gold example — the build fails on any drift.
Reach an outside system Connector — deliberately none Nothing external is touched while the analysis runs. Building one would only add a surface that can fail.

Governance change control (rules lived, not laminated)

VersionChangeTrigger
v1.0Five tests (EH-1/EH-2 historical, PP-1 pace vs population, CR-1 capacity, CX-1 context-only), class thresholds, High = ≥2 tests incl. ≥1 historical, max three signalsOwner approval before any signal existed
v1.1Documented ranking tie-break; truncation disclosure covers any status; CX-1 extended to mill rates; approved comparison classes made explicitCritic round 1
v1.2Subject excluded from its own comparator median; included municipalities named; Watch wording aligned with the implemented rule; not-flagged verdicts publish full evidenceCritic round 2
Section 2 of 6

Evidence of use

The workspace ran eight logged sessions against the real data (full log in outputs/run_log.md). The headline output — the 2025 pre-budget run under rules v1.2:

#SignalStatusTriggered tests (values shown in the deliverable)
S1Parks & recreation expense per capita — $295.97 (nominal)High review priority EH-1 (8-yr max), EH-2 (+20.70% / 3 yr vs 10% threshold), PP-1 (expense +39.24% vs population +15.36%), CX-1 context
S2Non-residential municipal mill rate — 24.22 millsHigh review priority EH-1 (8-yr max), EH-2 (+14.92% / 3 yr), CX-1 context (Calgary 17.97; AB median excl. Edmonton 13.42, n=18)
S3Police expense per capita — $509.96 (nominal)High review priority EH-1 (8-yr max), PP-1 (+24.67% vs +15.36% population), CX-1 context; EH-2 correctly not triggered (+8.08%)

A fourth High signal (fte_per_1000 — municipal FTE per 1,000 residents fell from 10.20 to 9.62) overflowed the three-signal cap and is disclosed by name and status everywhere, with full test evidence in the drill-down. debt_utilization (59.27% vs Calgary 36.34%) stayed a Watch: its only trigger is cross-sectional, and context evidence can never create High — the rule held exactly where it was designed to.

Selected episodes from the use log

  • The audit note that was wrong. My own pre-build audit counted 4 blank cells in the 2025 debt schedule; the first known-answer test failed against the pipeline. Investigation showed the blanks sit on a footer row that literally reads "306 out of 332" — the pipeline was right, the audit note was wrong. Failure home: Knowledge. Bonus: the coverage disclosure now cites the workbook itself.
  • Correct numbers, broken product. Using the dashboard as the budget manager surfaced two defects no value-check could see: comparison evidence rendering as raw JSON, and a status chip claiming "Not flagged" for a metric that was actually Watch. Both fixed and re-verified the same session.
  • The gate that blocks. Tampering the curated gold value to 42.0 made validate_mart.py exit 1 and name the broken anchor; rebuild restored green. The rule is enforced, not suggested.
  • One genuine missing value. Across all approved codes and years, exactly one true blank exists — Granum (0135), 2021 equalized assessment. It surfaces as missing, never as zero, and became the missing-vs-zero test anchor.
  • Edmonton was inside its own benchmark. The critic's sharpest catch: the Alberta-cities median included Edmonton, so one "peer" comparator was Edmonton's own mill rate. Rules v1.2 now excludes the subject from its own comparator and names every included municipality.
  • The group-project comparison that became a feature. A structured evaluation against my group’s 311 dashboard (2026-07-17) surfaced practices worth importing — an on-screen methodology view, a product brief, and its map technique. Governance held under the temptation: an Edmonton neighbourhood map was rejected (this data’s grain is municipal — 407 polygons would all display one number), and the approved adaptation is a province-level context map gated by a new owner-approved source, a scoped crosswalk extension with a written sign-off, and ten new tests — including one that fails the build if the map’s medians drift from the mart’s.

The management brief the workspace produces

Drafted by the municipal-finance-analyst subagent from validated outputs only, and gated:

draft — owner approval required before publishing

Edmonton Fiscal Pressure Review Brief — 2025

DRAFT — requires owner approval before publishing

1. Purpose and period

This brief supports pre-budget review prioritization for the City of Edmonton. It flags the financial pressure areas that most warrant deeper management analysis; it does not assess overall financial health. Evidence covers reporting years 2018–2025, with Edmonton's own history as the primary benchmark. 2025 coverage notice: 306 of 332 Alberta financial information returns received — all 2025 peer comparisons are provisional. Signals evaluate nominal dollar figures; an owner-approved constant-2025$ CPI display context (StatCan 18-10-0005-01, All-items Alberta; approved 2026-07-18) is available in the drill-down and never feeds a test. Signals were generated under pressure rules v1.2 (approved 2026-07-17), governance/pressure_rules.md; 22 metrics were evaluated.

2. Top signals (three surfaced; truncation disclosed below)

Signal 1 — Parks & recreation expense per capita: High review priority.

$295.97 per resident in 2025 (nominal), the most adverse value in the 2018–2025 history (2018: $278.34; 2020 low: $199.68). Triggered tests: EH-1 (2025 is the 8-year historical maximum, exceeding the prior high of $286.21 in 2024); EH-2 (+20.70% over 2022's $245.20, against a 10.0% threshold); PP-1 (parks & recreation expense grew 39.24% over three years while population grew 15.36% — a 23.88 pp adverse gap against a 5.0 pp threshold). Supporting context only (CX-1, provisional): versus Calgary's 2025 value of $179.36, an adverse gap of 65.02% (relative to the comparator) against a 15% threshold. Per-capita spending in this function has moved ahead of both its own history and population growth — the widest test exceedances in this run.

Signal 2 — Non-residential general municipal mill rate: High review priority.

24.22 mills in 2025, the highest of the eight years on record (2018: 17.44). Triggered tests: EH-1 (2025 exceeds the prior high of 22.66 in 2024); EH-2 (+14.92% over 2022's 21.08, against a 10.0% threshold). Supporting context only (CX-1, provisional): versus Calgary's 2025 rate of 17.97, an adverse gap of 34.77% (relative to the comparator) against a 15% threshold; and versus the Alberta reporting-city median of 13.4191 mills — computed excluding Edmonton per rules v1.2 (n=18: Airdrie, Beaumont, Brooks, Calgary, Camrose, Fort Saskatchewan, Grande Prairie, Lacombe, Leduc, Lethbridge, Lloydminster, Medicine Hat, Red Deer, Spruce Grove, St. Albert, Wetaskiwin, Chestermere, Cold Lake) — an adverse gap of 80.51% (relative to the comparator) against a 15% threshold. The rate has risen in six of the last seven years and its recent movement exceeded the documented threshold.

Signal 3 — Police expense per capita: High review priority.

$509.96 per resident in 2025 (nominal), the highest in the 2018–2025 history (2018: $443.20). Triggered tests: EH-1 (2025 exceeds the prior high of $490.56 in 2024); PP-1 (police expense grew 24.67% over three years versus 15.36% population growth — a 9.32 pp adverse gap against a 5.0 pp threshold). EH-2 did not trigger (+8.08% over three years, below the 10.0% threshold). Supporting context only (CX-1, provisional): versus Calgary's 2025 value of $428.21, an adverse gap of 19.09% (relative to the comparator) against a 15% threshold. Growth outpaced population, though the 3-year percentage movement alone stayed under threshold.

Truncated signals — 10 additional flagged signals not shown, per the three-signal limit (name and status): fte_per_1000 (High review priority); mill_rate_res (Watch); exp_fire_per_capita (Watch); exp_transit_per_capita (Watch); assessment_per_capita (Watch); exp_per_capita (Watch); debt_service_utilization (Watch); debt_utilization (Watch); exp_culture_per_capita (Watch); exp_roads_per_capita (Watch). Full test evidence for each is retained in data/curated/signals.json. Nine further metrics were not flagged (including rev_per_capita, net_financial_assets, and accumulated_surplus), with their full test evidence also published.

3. Evidence and source traces

All values come from the validated outputs data/curated/signals.json and data/curated/metric_mart.json; every curated value carries its source file, worksheet (schedule), variable code, and year. Extraction uses schedule + variable code (never column position); Edmonton's financial code is 0098. Traces for the signals above:

  • Parks & recreation expense per capita — derived exp_parks_rec / pop; numerator: annual _financial_year.xlsx workbooks, sheet C(1)-Revenue, code 01530; denominator: population-estimates-ab-census-subdivision-municipal-2016-to-current.xlsx, CSD 4811061 (the only approved population source).
  • Non-residential mill rate — annual _financial_year.xlsx workbooks, sheet MR(3)-Mill Rate, code 10034. Alberta median rows in the mart name every included municipality and are computed excluding the subject (rules v1.2).
  • Police expense per capita — derived exp_police / pop; numerator: sheet C(1)-Revenue, code 01210; denominator as above.

Edmonton's own 2018–2025 history is the primary benchmark. Calgary (code 0046) and Alberta reporting-city figures are supporting context only and can never create a high-priority signal.

4. Limitations

  • Signal tests evaluate nominal dollars (rules v1.2). Real-dollar interpretation is supported only through the owner-approved constant-2025$ CPI display context (StatCan 18-10-0005-01, All-items Alberta, base 2025; approved 2026-07-18) shown in the drill-down — for example, Edmonton's 2018 expense per capita of $3,020.55 nominal is $3,699.43 in 2025 dollars, so the nominal 8.1% rise over 2018–2025 is a decline in real terms. Nominal test triggers are not evidence of real cost growth.
  • No forecasting. With eight annual observations per metric, a predictive model would be statistically indefensible. A governed path exists — a longer approved return history, an owner-approved method with mandatory uncertainty reporting, and its own review gate — and remains future work outside this brief.
  • 2025 peer comparisons are provisional (306 of 332 returns received). Alberta reporting-city medians exclude Edmonton (the subject is never part of its own comparator, rules v1.2) and name the included municipalities (2025: n=18).
  • Findings are descriptive: no causal claims, no forecasts, no assessment of financial health, and no aggregation of signals into a score.
  • Thresholds, tie-break rules, and comparator composition are documented analytical heuristics in governance/pressure_rules.md (v1.2, approved 2026-07-17), not City policy.

5. Follow-up questions for management

  • What drove the 39.24% three-year growth in parks & recreation expense — new facilities, service expansion, contract costs, or reporting changes — and is the pace expected to persist?
  • Which components of the non-residential mill-rate increase reflect deliberate rate decisions versus assessment-base movement, and how does this interact with the assessment_per_capita Watch flag and the provisional gaps versus Calgary and the Edmonton-excluded Alberta median?
  • What portions of police expense growth are settlement-driven, headcount-driven, or one-time, and how does the Calgary gap change once 2025 peer reporting is complete?
  • Should the truncated High review priority signal (fte_per_1000) be pulled into the next review cycle alongside these three?
  • Are any 2025 values affected by reporting or schedule changes that would alter comparability before conclusions are drawn?

This brief does not make policy recommendations. It flags areas for further analysis and management attention.

Section 3 of 6

Dashboard screenshots

The interface is a single static HTML file reading two generated data bundles (data.js, geo.js) — no server; the map view adds one vendored library (Leaflet 1.9.4) for its base tiles. Five views mirror the deliverable shape in the spec; the executive review still leads with at most three signals, never a KPI wall. The map and methodology views were added in the final owner-approved iteration (2026-07-17), importing the strongest practices from my group project's dashboard under this workspace's own governance gates. The same dashboard is published live at alecruz-cloud.github.io/edmonton-fiscal-pressure-navigator (GitHub Pages, built from the project repository), so every screenshot below can be verified interactively.

Section 4 of 6

Evaluation — test set, scoring, before and after

Per the course method, the evaluation tests outputs, not code. Numbers are checked by independent recomputation (pytest known-answer tests plus a critic that re-extracts from raw with its own code); narrative outputs are reviewed by me, never scored by another model. Full record: outputs/evaluation.md.

Baseline test set (from CLAUDE.md)

CaseExpectedResult
Gold example (Edmonton 2025)pop 1,238,295 · debt $4,592,150,000 · limit $7,748,380,000 · utilization 59.27% ±0.01 pp · expense $4,043,938,000 · $3,265.73/capita ±$0.01 · full traces · population from estimates workbook onlyPass
Two extra known answers (other domains)2022 debt utilization 64.11% · 2025 FTE/1,000 = 9.6237Pass
Missing vs zeroGranum 2021 assessment stays missing; 209 zeros in the 2025 debt schedule stay reported_zero; never mergedPass after correction — the test's own expected count was wrong (footer row); failure home: Knowledge
Provisional peer coverage2025 peers labelled provisional (306 of 332); included municipalities counted and namedPass
Must-not-flag casedebt_utilization = Watch (context alone can't create High); rev_per_capita = Not flaggedPass
Narrative causation/policy checkNo causal, policy, health, or score language anywherePass (automated ban-list + owner review; both critic rounds found zero instances)
Source-trace completenessEvery mart row: workbook › schedule › variable code › year (or derived formula)Pass (752/752 rows)
Cold run of User Story 1≤3 signals with reasons, verifiable on screen by a cold userPass after corrections — two UI defects found by use, fixed, re-verified; critic replayed the story cold in both rounds

The independent critic — the same brief, run twice

The critic runs in fresh context, banned from the build history, playing the budget manager cold. It must attack the spec's promises against the running deliverables, recompute numbers from raw with its own code, and classify every finding's home. I triaged every finding — accept, reject, or defer, with reasons.

Round 1 → 9 findings, all accepted

  • Truncated High signal had no inspectable evidence (Deliverables)
  • Third slot decided by dict order — undocumented tie-break (Governance/Skills)
  • Brief hid truncated statuses (Deliverables)
  • Truncation wording assumed only Watch overflows (Governance)
  • structurally_unavailable was dead vocabulary (Skills/Spec)
  • classify() gaps; context shown without validity gate; mill-rate median unused; two latent code defects

Fold-back: rules v1.1 + engine/dashboard fixes + 5 regression tests.

Round 2 → 8 findings (2 resolved-by-race, 6 accepted)

  • Brief comparison phrasing & version — already fixed by regeneration running concurrently (recorded honestly as resolved-before-triage)
  • CX-only Watch explanations self-contradictory (Skills)
  • Not-flagged verdicts unverifiable (Deliverables)
  • The median included Edmonton itself (Governance → v1.2)
  • reported_zero dropped from signal histories — latent (Skills)
  • Stale hardcoded UI claims (Deliverables)

Fold-back: rules v1.2 + fixes + 4 regression tests.

What held under both adversarial rounds: the gold example and every published signal value recomputed exactly from raw by independent code; every High had ≥2 tests including a historical one; context never created High; missing ≠ zero held at its real edge case; the stale financial-workbook population never leaked into any figure; raw files unchanged; language discipline everywhere. Round 2 found no Living Spec and no Knowledge findings at all.

Before → after

ReadingBaselineAfter round 1After round 2After map iteration (2026-07-17)
Automated tests19 pass / 1 fail37 pass41 pass / 0 fail51 pass / 0 fail
Critic findings open96 new0 accepted findings open0 open · critic not re-run for this display-only iteration
Pressure rulesv1.0v1.1v1.2 — every change owner-approved, dated, loggedv1.2 unchanged — the map adds no test, threshold, or signal
Verifiable outcomesflagged signals only+ truncated signalsall 22 evaluated metrics, every verdict+ the map’s per-city values verified to reproduce the mart medians
Section 5 of 6

Write-up

The user and the work

Alberta publishes a dense, genuinely good dataset — every municipality's Financial Information Return, every year — that almost nobody reads before decisions get made, because reading it means pivoting fifty worksheets of variable codes. The person who should read it is easy to name: the budget manager who, two days before a review cycle, must choose where analytical attention goes. Their real constraint is not data access but defensibility — any number they surface will be challenged, so a signal they cannot trace is worse than no signal. That constraint shaped everything: the workspace's core promise is not "insight" but at most three flags, every figure wearing its provenance, every threshold documented, every verdict — including "nothing to see here" — verifiable on screen.

What I refused to build

The rejected alternative (recorded in the spec) was a generic Alberta fiscal-health ranking dashboard. Municipalities differ in scale, responsibilities and reporting coverage; a composite score would demand arbitrary weights and invite false equivalence — a tour of the dataset instead of a decision. The same judgment recurs throughout: no forecasting, no causal claims, no policy recommendations, no healthy/unhealthy labels, and a hard rule that cross-sectional comparison alone can never create a high-priority signal. Edmonton's own eight-year history is the only benchmark strong enough to flag on.

How it is built

Plan. A read-only audit verified every data assumption before any code: Edmonton code 0098 in all eight workbooks, stable variable codes for every approved metric, the gold example reproduced by hand, and the population trap confirmed (the financial workbooks repeat a stale 1,010,899 for 2023–2025 — the estimates workbook shows 1,238,295 for 2025). The spec and an HTML walking skeleton fixed the deliverable shape before the build; the skeleton's faked panels became the capability list.

Build. Everything deterministic became a fixed pipeline; everything judgment-shaped became a gated capability. The two skills carry blocking code checks (proven to block on tampered input, not just to pass on good input). The two subagents isolate the two judgments worth isolating — cautious narration, and adversarial critique — with the analyst structurally unable to recompute numbers or publish without my approval. The dashboard is deliberately boring technology: one static page, one generated data file, no server.

Use. Eight logged runs produced the 2025 result above, and the log's honest column is the more valuable one: my own audit note was wrong once; the product broke twice in ways correct numbers cannot reveal; and generated prose failed the same way UI does (a Watch explanation claiming nothing triggered). Each episode landed as a fix plus a regression test, not just a patch.

Evaluate. The strongest course idea — the checker cannot be the author — did the most work here. A fresh-context critic attacked the spec's promises twice and recomputed everything from raw. Its findings were specific enough to act on within hours, and the sharpest one (Edmonton sitting inside its own peer median) was a genuine analytical flaw I had not seen. Every finding crossed my triage with a written verdict; three of the fifteen accepted findings were fixed in governance text, not code — the diagram's reminder that the home of a defect is often not the app.

What the evaluation showed

Numbers were never the problem — both critic rounds matched every published value against raw to four decimals. What failed, repeatedly, was the product around the numbers: evidence a user could not reach, disclosures that hid a status, prose that contradicted its own verdict, a benchmark quietly containing its subject. That is precisely what output-level evaluation with an independent critic is for, and why the before/after reads 19-pass baseline → 41-pass, rules v1.0 → v1.2, "flagged signals verifiable" → "every verdict verifiable."

The last iteration — importing my group project’s best practices

On the final day I evaluated this workspace against my group’s Edmonton 311 dashboard and imported what survived this project’s governance: a five-view dashboard (small-multiples explorer, an on-screen methodology view rendering rules v1.2 with the worked gold example, a verdict board for all 22 metrics), a PRODUCT.md brief, and an Alberta context map. The map is the disciplined translation of the group’s choropleth: their data is neighbourhood-grained; mine is municipal, so an Edmonton neighbourhood map would be decoration — the honest spatial unit here is the province’s 19 reporting cities. Every gate the spec demands was exercised for real: the StatCan 2021 boundary file was owner-approved and stored read-only in data/raw/; the crosswalk grew from 2 to 19 rows under an explicit geo-only scope with a written sign-off; per-capita peer values remain structurally unavailable; and ten new tests pin the map to the mart — its embedded per-city values must reproduce the published medians and the gold example or the build fails. The dashboard stayed boring technology — static files, no server — with one deliberate, owner-approved exception on the final day (2026-07-18): the map view renders on Carto/OSM base tiles through a single vendored library (Leaflet 1.9.4) so the geography reads instantly. The tiles are presentation only — on-screen attribution, zoom capped at region scale so centroid bubbles are never mistaken for addresses, an offline fallback to the StatCan outline, and every value still coming solely from geo.js.

Closing the nominal-dollar limitation — and declining to forecast

Reading the brief's own limitations prompted two owner decisions on the final day (2026-07-18). First, the inflation adjustment the assessment had reserved as "an optional future Knowledge Stock addition" was exercised: StatCan table 18-10-0005-01 (All-items Alberta CPI, 2002=100) became approved Source D, and every Edmonton dollar metric gained a *_real2025 sibling — computed in the pipeline with hard guards, pinned by six new tests (57/57 green, validator anchors 8→10), and rendered only through a labelled drill-down toggle that hides peer series so two dollar bases never share an axis. Signals still evaluate nominal values under rules v1.2; the context reframes without re-scoring — nominal expense per capita rose 8.1% over 2018–2025 while CPI rose 22.5%, a real-terms decline the manager can now see with its full trace. Second, a requested ML forecast was evaluated and refused: eight annual observations per metric cannot support a defensible predictive model, and the spec now records the governed path any future proposal must bring (longer approved history, an approved method with mandatory uncertainty reporting, its own review gate). One number in this iteration was caught wrong before it shipped: a from-memory "$3,019.86" for 2018 expense per capita failed verification against the mart ($3,020.55) — the project's first load-bearing rule applied to its own author.

Limitations and what I would do next

  • Signals still evaluate nominal dollars. The inflation index this list once named as the highest-value addition shipped on the final day exactly as prescribed — a governed source, display-only, never a silent adjustment. Moving the tests to real dollars would be a rules-v1.3 decision: re-calibrated thresholds, owner approval, and a critic re-run.
  • 2025 peer evidence stays provisional until the remaining 26 returns arrive; the workspace already labels this everywhere and the label lifts automatically when a future workbook closes the gap.
  • The eligible-metric set is deliberately narrow (22). Widening it (e.g., debt service by purpose, salary schedules added in 2023+) is a metric-dictionary decision with owner approval, not a code edit.
  • A third signal class — schedule-drift alerts when a variable code disappears — would turn today's fail-fast RuntimeError into a governed structurally_unavailable pathway.
Standing boundary: the workspace flags areas for review with evidence. It does not make policy recommendations, does not assess Edmonton as financially healthy or unhealthy, and does not replace professional financial review or Council accountability.
Section 6 of 6

Distinctness statement

Dataset: Government of Alberta open data — the annual Municipal Financial and Statistical Data workbooks (2018–2025) and the Alberta Census Subdivision Population Estimates. Problem: pre-budget prioritization of Edmonton's financial pressure areas for a municipal budget manager.

My group's project was built on Edmonton 311 service-request data and served an operations question: monitoring service-request volumes and prioritizing operational response. This final differs on both required axes. The dataset is different in kind, not just in name — provincial financial returns and demographic estimates rather than a municipal service-ticket feed — and the problem is different in domain, user, and decision: corporate finance and budget-review prioritization for a financial-planning manager, resting on longitudinal fiscal evidence, versus operational workload monitoring for a service-operations lead. No data, code, or analysis is shared between the two projects. The final iteration deliberately imported two practices from the group work — a written product brief and the idea of a geographic context view — but implemented them against a different boundary source (StatCan census subdivisions, not Edmonton neighbourhood polygons), a different spatial unit (municipalities, not neighbourhoods), and this project’s own pipeline, rules, and tests.

Edmonton Fiscal Pressure Navigator · MMA 616 Individual Final · Alejandro Cruz · 2026-07-18. Every figure in this document traces to workbook › schedule › variable code › year in the workspace's curated outputs. This document reports the workspace; it makes no policy recommendations.
